solution.p: We will factor y^{2}+2y+1 where a=1, b=2, and c=1.

Perfect Square Formula: The formula for perfect square trinomials is (a \pm b)^2=a^2 \pm 2ab + b^2. Note that the polynomial y^{2}+2y+1 matches the formula's right side, and we have: \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}+2y+1\amp =\left(y\right)^2+2\left(y\right)\left(1\right)+\left(1\right)^2\newline \amp =\left(y+1\right)\mathopen{}\left(y+1\right)\newline \amp =\left(y+1\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.

For y^{2}+2y+1, since the product of 1 and 1 is 1, and the sum of 1 and 1 is 2, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}+2y+1\amp =\left(y+1\right)\mathopen{}\left(y+1\right)\newline \amp =\left(y+1\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (1) = 1.

The number 1 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(1)\amp -1(-1)\amp \end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of 2, and this sum happens with 1+1=2 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]y^{2}+2y+1\amp =y^{2}+y+y+1
ewline \amp =y\mathopen{}\left(y+1ight)+y+1
ewline \amp =\left(y+1ight)\mathopen{}\left(y+1ight)
ewline \amp =\left(y+1ight)^{2}\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in y^{2}+2y+1 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}
The third term, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp -1(-1)\amp \end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with y^{2}+2y+1 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:

Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}+2y+1\amp =\left(y+1\right)\mathopen{}\left(y+1\right)\newline \amp =\left(y+1\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
